videos.title: Never Mix Up The US and Canada Again
How to tell USA & Canada apart
Asking GeoGuessr Devs Their Best USA Tips

hints.text: Speed signs say **"SPEED LIMIT"** in **miles** — numbers lower than Canada's, often ending in 5

shared_with.note: Canada writes 'MAXIMUM' and uses km/h (higher numbers).

hints.text: US signs are **wordy**: railway crossings literally say 'RAILROAD CROSSING'; warning text sits ON the yellow diamond

shared_with.note: Canadian crossbucks are typically blank; warning words go on a small rectangle BELOW a diagram diamond.

hints.text: **American spelling** (center, color) + **imperial units**: miles, feet & inches on bridge-height signs

shared_with.note: English Canada uses British spelling (centre, colour) and metric.

hints.text: Yellow triangle **'No Passing Zone'** signs are exclusive to the US
**Rectangular junction signs** w/ single/double arrow
Yield signs: red border, white centre **+ the word 'YIELD'**
hints.uniq_note: Canadian yield signs look identical but have NO text — text = US.

hints.text: One-way signs: black rectangle w/ white arrow, always **'ONE WAY'** printed (cities: white vertical version)

shared_with.note: Same shape/colour, but may lack the 'ONE WAY' text.

hints.text: White & orange **fibre-optic cable markers** along roads
hints.uniq_note: Almost unique to the US — very rarely seen in Canada.

hints.text: **Yellow middle + white outer lines**; double yellow middle most common; on divided roads the line nearest the median is yellow

shared_with.note: Double middle lines are less common in Canada, but not rare — caution.

hints.text: **Rumble strips** (small perpendicular indentations) on interstate/US-highway edges & between lanes

shared_with.note: Rumble strips exist in Canada but are much rarer.

hints.text: **Mobile/trailer homes** popular in rural areas (most common Southeast, found everywhere)

shared_with.note: Seen in Canada to a much lesser extent.

hints.text: **Baptist churches** very common (esp. Southern Plains & Southeast, but in all 50 states)

shared_with.note: Baptist churches are rare in Canada.

hints.text: Highway exits: large green metal signs w/ **curved edges**; blue **services signs** (food/lodging/gas) before exits

shared_with.note: Canadian highway signs similar but typically square-edged; services signs on some highways too.

hints.text: **Gen2 is rare** in the US → a North American Gen2 round is more likely Canada (exceptions: Alaska, some Hawaii, rarely Deep South); Gen3 less common than in Canada; Gen3 **side-mirror blur = single peak** (Canada: two peaks)
Parking signs: white metal w/ red/green/black text, **wordy** rules + arrows

shared_with.note: Similar style but less wordy, more symbols.

hints.text: Orange **construction barrels** w/ horizontal white stripes + black rubber base
**North America vs Europe tells** (US + Canada):
bullets: Post mailboxes w/ little side flag
Wooden utility poles + **large cylindrical metal transformers** (never in Europe)
Slightly raised insulator on top of simple poles
**Short licence plates**, no blue strip
Long metal bars w/ stoplights hanging over intersections
Thin metal signposts w/ small holes or U-profile
Long-nose semi-trucks (Europe/Asia = flat-front cabovers)
Tall water towers, sometimes w/ town name painted on
Gen3 usually has **no antenna** (US national parks: stubby coiled antenna — never in Europe)
Gen4 'smallcam' exists: low camera, big round blur, right mirror sticking out

hints.text: **Population density**: east of the Missouri River much denser than the west (clickable map)
Each state has a **unique plate design** (some readable through blur); about **half the states don't require a front plate** — rear-only skews South & East (map)
**Interstates**: even = W–E (lowest along Gulf/Mexico), odd = S–N (lowest at West Coast). **US Highways** numbered roughly inversely (low odd = East Coast, low even = north) — more exceptions there
Phone numbers start w/ a 3-digit **area code** — not clustered, but learnable (map)
Each state flies its own **state flag** — the distinct ones appear often (overview)

**Topography**: high west (Rockies, plateaus; low Central & Imperial Valley in CA) vs low east (Appalachians the main relief; hills also in the Driftless Area + Ozarks); **forest density**: east & north much denser than west/southwest
**Landscape regions**:
bullets: Great Plains (Canada→central TX): flat, few trees — fields or dry grass
Appalachians: low forested mountains, beech & oak; southern houses lower, New England style toward the north
Rockies (MT→northern NM): jagged rocky peaks, pines, year-round snow on top; some dry parts
Great Basin (NV core): tall (snow-capped) mountains behind dry grassy/shrubby plains — gentler rocky hills in OR, greener in ID/central UT
Deep South (OK→SC): reddish soil (yellow→deep red)
Prairie potholes (MT/SK border→western Great Lakes): marshy grass + small shallow ponds
Blue Ridge (S-NY→S-VA): forested rolling ridges SW–NE + flat valleys
Lower Mississippi Valley: very flat, open, red soil, corn (TN/AR/MS/NE-LA)
Ouachita Mtns (E-OK→W-AR): steeper than the nearby Ozarks
Four Corners (UT/AZ/NM/CO): red mesas & tablelands + red soil
Pacific coast (WA/OR/N-CA): temperate rainforest — broadleaf + huge conifers + ferns
Eastern WA (+N/E OR): golden agricultural fields
CA Central Valley: Mediterranean — vineyards, olives, rolling edges
TX Hill Country (W of Austin/San Antonio): dry rocky hills, yucca, prickly pear, live oaks
Rio Grande Valley (S-TX): extremely flat, shrubby trees + fields, Tamaulipas border

hints.text: **Crops**:
bullets: Corn: Midwest corn belt (see map)
Soybeans (short dark-green rows): Eastern Plains, Midwest, Lower Mississippi, Mid-Atlantic
Canola (yellow flowers): North Dakota along the Canadian border (rarely MT)
Cotton (fluffy white): coastal SE, Lower Mississippi, W-TX, parts of CA/AZ
Rice (flat low prairies): ONLY north-central CA + Lower Mississippi (AR/LA/TX/MS) — great region tell

hints.text: **Flora**:
bullets: Eastern White Pine (layered branches curving up): New England, northern Midwest, Appalachians
Southern pines (tall, fluffy needle clusters): the Southeast signature tree
Southern lawns (Bermuda/St. Augustine grass) turn beige in winter; northern lawns stay green
Sagebrush (blue-green shrub): arid interior West
Douglas firs (extremely tall, dark): Cascadia + N-CA/ID/MT (also BC!)
Fan palms: SE coasts + desert Southwest (Mexican Fan Palm: CA + Gulf Coast)
Spanish moss (grey-white drapes on trees): Deep South

hints.text: **State infrastructure IDs**: each state has its own **highway shield** + distinct **Adopt-a-Highway** sign designs; blue-yellow **county road shields** in certain states; **US National Parks paint guardrails/signs/posts brown** (wood or metal, sometimes rock walls)
**Road-surface state metas**:
bullets: Rectangular raised pavement markers (RPMs): south + west + Massachusetts
Snowplowable 'H'-shaped markers: eastern highway states only
RPMs BESIDE the double yellow (not between): GA, FL, TX, CA
Extra-wide gap between yellow lines: TX, AR, LA, MS, OR
Grainy 'salt & pepper' surface: Texas (also nearby NM/OK/CO)
Grainy rural roads in the Upper Midwest: most common Illinois
Black stripe between the yellows: California
No middle line, white edges only: small roads in CA & KY
Thicker yellow middle lines: WV, MD, DE, VA

hints.text: **Multi-state sign metas**:
bullets: Signs on TWO posts: MI, OH, MN, NY (most common)
Coloured driveway signs w/ house number + town/road name: WI & MI peninsula (neighbours: numbers only)
White intersection signs: Virginia (and North Carolina)
Black street signs w/ white text: South Carolina
Green/red bridge & guardrail end markers: ME, NH, MA
NY/VT small green signs w/ 3 number rows: VT 2nd row starts 0; NY 2nd row = NYSDOT district

hints.text: **New England kit**: short roadside **rock walls** (CT/RI/MA/NH); coastal **shingle-wall** houses; urban **three-deckers** (NY/CT/RI/MA — also Atlantic Canada); **brownstone** row houses (NE cities + Chicago)
**Architecture by region**:
bullets: Stilt houses: southern Atlantic & Gulf coasts
Short concrete/wood stilts (1–3 ft): western/central Gulf Coast
Very large sloped roofs: OK, TX, LA
Red metal roofs: Mid-South & southern Appalachia (VA/WV/OH/KY/TN/AR/NC)
Spanish Colonial Revival (light stucco + red clay tiles): desert SW (CA/NV/AZ/TX) + FL/coastal GA
Dark wooden boards + metal roofs: interior mountain West

hints.text: **Pole metas**: 3 yellow base stripes → most of California (exc. Modoc/Lassen; also Grants Pass, OR); white plate w/ orange rectangle + diamond → Wisconsin & Michigan's Upper Peninsula; yellow **PacifiCorp** plates → OR, E-ID, UT, WY (rarer CA/WA)
**Store & restaurant chains** (region deciders):
bullets: US-only gas & food chains exist that Canada lacks (see graphics; Chick-fil-A the near-only exception)
ShopRite: CT/NY/NJ/PA/DE/MD · Hy-Vee: IA/MN/WI/IL · Piggly Wiggly: SE + WI
H-E-B: Texas only · Publix: FL + Deep South · Whataburger: TX-centred South
Waffle House: South/SE (GA #1) · Zaxby's: SE (GA #1, pockets in UT/MT)
Carl's Jr. = West Coast/SW vs Hardee's = central/East (same logo! Both only in WY & OK)
Pizza Ranch: IA/MN · In-N-Out: SW (2/3 in CA) · Tim Hortons (US side): near ON/QC border
WaWa: Mid-Atlantic + FL (NJ/Philly densest) · Sheetz: PA/Mid-Atlantic/Appalachia
Kwik Trip: WI/MN ('Kwik Star' in IA) · Kum & Go: IA + Ozarks/CO/WY/ND · Casey's: Midwest/Plains (IL/IA/MO) · Buc-ee's (oversized stations): TX

hints.text: **Non-contiguous US** (all have their own pages on this site): Alaska (northern-/western-/easternmost state), Hawaii (only state outside N. America), Puerto Rico (Caribbean Commonwealth), American Samoa, Guam, Northern Marianas, US Virgin Islands (**the only left-driving part of the US**), US Minor Outlying Islands (Midway)
**Saguaro cacti** (tall tubes w/ branching arms) → southern Arizona, Sonoran border
Utility poles w/ **3 yellow stripes** at the base (most of CA, exc. Modoc/Lassen NE)
hints.uniq_note: Also in Grants Pass, Oregon — otherwise a CA lock.

hints.text: **Front Range**: N–S mountain ridge flanking Denver/Boulder/Colorado Springs — Rockies west, Great Plains east
Central FL (Orlando↔Lakeland) = the only real **rolling hills** area, w/ lakes + fields
**Square-reflector bollard** (also UT/AZ) — Idaho appears w/ both 1 and 2 bolts (UT usually 2, AZ 1)
Grainy **salt-and-pepper rural roads** of the Upper Midwest are most common in Illinois (colour varies)
**Concrete paving** on ordinary rural roads much more common than elsewhere (ignore bridges/highways/cities)
hints.uniq_note: Only trust it on plain rural roads.

hints.text: **White reflectors on signposts** (esp. rural stop signs) — unique to Kansas
Uniquely-shaped **rumble strips**: thin, long recessed lines on the very outside road edges
**Shotgun houses** (very narrow 1-storey wood) → New Orleans area (also Houston, Louisville, Mississippi)
Stoplights & signs **hang from wires** across big intersections, often in yellow boxes; signs often on two posts (also OH/MN/NY)
hints.uniq_note: Not exclusive, but by far most common in Michigan.

hints.text: **Yellow signs w/ vertical black triangles** before bridges & guardrail ends; horizontal black-white marker on rural stop-sign posts
County highways lettered **A–ZZZ** (only WI does this too)
hints.uniq_note: Wisconsin usually writes 'COUNTY' above the letter — Missouri doesn't.

hints.text: **Sandhills**: rolling prairie-grass dunes in the central-north of the state
Bollard = **white vertical-rectangle reflector w/ black outline** on a thin stick
White metal **'E-2' sign** beside the road, typically at bridge ends
Bollard w/ **3 circular reflectors**; **Pueblo-Revival adobe** houses (rounded edges, flat roofs, sand-brown — also AZ/CO to a lesser extent)
**Square guardrails** (long top bar on square legs) dominant (also Wyoming); small green 3-row number signs (2nd row = NYSDOT district; Vermont's start w/ 0)
Street signs showing **street numbers + arrows** alongside the name; plain white signs on posts common too
**Green sign w/ 3 rows of numbers** on the back of rural stop signs
Speed signs often just say **'SPEED'**; bollard = very long rectangular reflector on top (similar in CA)
White rectangular signs at intersections w/ the **converging state-route number** on top
**Black street signs w/ white text** common at intersections
**White diamond-reflector bollard**, usually **2 bolts** (Montana = 1 bolt; design exists elsewhere — caution, squares can be rotated)
**Yellow/red 'ketchup & mustard' bands** around signposts; grainy salt-and-pepper roads; huge sloped roofs (also OK/LA); oil pumpjacks (also E-NM/ND)
Bollard = **white square reflector atop a black rectangle** (unique to Utah); black paddle bollards (also WY/NE); streets named like **'600 South'** (also ID)
Small **green 3-row number signs** — 2nd row almost always starts w/ **0** (New York's = NYSDOT district number)
hints.uniq_note: Same sign type as NY — the 0 is the Vermont tell.

hints.text: White rectangular signs at intersections; **thicker yellow middle lines** (also WV/MD/DE)
Bollard = white/brown post, **white rectangle front + two small squares on the back**; golden wheat fields in the east
Street signs w/ a **fraction-in-a-circle** symbol
Simple **white bollards w/ black reflectors** (unique); green **ATV Route** signs (unique); orange rectangle+diamond pole plate (also MI UP); county letters w/ the word 'COUNTY'
Bollard: dark post, white circular reflector w/ the post rising **above** it — top sometimes painted **white/grey (unique)**; black paddle bollard w/ white square reflector = WY-only variant

**Landscape spotlights**: Everglades (jungle-straight lonely roads Ft. Myers↔Miami); Florida Keys (single-lane highway, tropical green, white sandy soil); Wichita Mtns (SW-OK, pink-red rocky peaks); Guadalupe Mtns (NM/TX border, tall dry ridge); Black Hills (W-SD/E-WY, conifer mountains in flat surroundings)
**Ford Bronco** (unblurred black roof, red hood, 2 escorting Ford SUVs behind) on unpaved rural roads = TransAmerica-Trail promo coverage, Oregon → North Carolina
**Interstate 19** (Tucson → Mexico border) is the only US highway signed in **kilometres** (speed limits still MPH)

**City tells**: Pittsburgh = yellow steel bridges + 3 rivers + bluffs; San Francisco = colourful Victorian row houses w/ bay windows; New Orleans = colourful shotgun houses + wrap-around balconies; Miami (Beach) = bright art-deco blocks near the coast
**National parks**:
bullets: Dry Tortugas (W of Key West): hexagonal brick fort + moat; Loggerhead lighthouse brown/white
Big Bend (SW-TX): dry rocky peaks, yucca + prickly pear (Chihuahuan desert)
Death Valley: dry sandy treeless mountains, dusty Gen3 sky
Glacier (MT/Canada border): extremely tall steep peaks, hazy summer coverage
Badlands (SD): sand-brown eroded formations
Theodore Roosevelt (ND): same but w/ more trees + fall colours
Devils Tower (NE-WY): lone giant butte visible for miles
